system_prompt: You are an analyst. Read the brief carefully and provide your best-reasoned recommendation.
user_prompt:
You are presented with 5 anonymous responses (E1-E5) and 12 bipolar constructs. Rate each response on each construct on a 1-7 scale: 1 = strongly the LEFT pole, 7 = strongly the RIGHT pole, 4 = neutral or mixed.

Responses:
**E1:** # Recommendation: Option C — Ecosystem-based adaptation as primary strategy

I recommend Option C as the primary allocation of the ~$17.5B/year climate spend, with the residual 30% baseline preparedness deliberately weighted toward hard infrastructure for the highest-value urban nodes (ports, capital flood barriers) and targeted managed retreat from the most indefensible zones. **Why ecosystem-based adaptation.** With 4500 km of coastline and a 15-year horizon, no plausible budget can armor the country in concrete. Hard seawalls cost roughly 3x per km and protect a static line against a moving...

**E2:** # Recommendation: Option C — Ecosystem-Based Adaptation as Primary Strategy

I recommend Option C as the primary strategy, with the understanding that the residual 30% baseline budget should be deliberately weighted toward hard infrastructure at the highest-value chokepoints (major ports, capital city flood barriers). **Why ecosystem-based adaptation?**

The country's defining physical fact is 4,500 km of coastline — far too much to seawall economically. Hard infrastructure at ~$50-100M/km would exhaust the entire 15-year budget protecting perhaps 10-15% of the coast, while leaving rural, agri...

**E3:** # Recommendation: Option C — Ecosystem-based adaptation as primary strategy

I recommend Option C as the anchor for the 70% allocation, with the residual 30% baseline preparedness deliberately weighted toward hard infrastructure for the highest-value urban nodes (ports, capital, critical grid) and targeted retreat from the most indefensible zones. **Why ecosystem-based adaptation.** With 4,500 km of coastline and only ~$17.5B/year in directed spending, the country cannot afford to wall its way out. Hard infrastructure runs roughly 3x the per-km cost of restored mangroves, wetlands, and reefs, ...

**E4:** # Recommendation: Option C — Ecosystem-based adaptation as primary strategy

I recommend Option C as the anchor for the 70% allocation, supplemented by the mandatory 30% baseline (which should absorb critical hard-infrastructure needs around the highest-value ports, capitals, and grid nodes). **Why ecosystem-based adaptation.** With 4,500 km of coastline and a $17.5B/year primary budget, the arithmetic of Option A is brutal: engineered seawalls run $10-50M per km, meaning hard defenses can credibly protect only a fraction of the coast before the budget is exhausted — and that fraction gets loc...

**E5:** # Recommendation: Option C — Ecosystem-based adaptation as primary strategy

With ~$17.5B/year (70% of the climate budget) directed by the primary strategy, the binding question is not "what protects the most assets today?" but "what compounds value across a 15-year horizon of deep uncertainty?" Ecosystem-based adaptation (EbA) wins on that question. **Why EbA.** A 4,500 km coastline cannot be feasibly armored end-to-end; cost-per-km math forces triage under any hard-infrastructure plan. Mangroves, wetlands, reefs, and restored watersheds deliver protection at roughly 30% the cost of seawalls,...

Constructs (id : left pole ↔ right pole):
  C_M1_1: 'Frames choice through cost-per-km arithmetic and triage'  (1) ↔ (7)  'Frames choice through long-horizon compounding value under uncertainty'
  C_M1_2: 'Explicit quantitative cost ratios for hard infrastructure'  (1) ↔ (7)  'Qualitative multiplier framing of relative costs'
  C_M1_3: 'Leads with budget math and per-km cost comparisons'  (1) ↔ (7)  'Leads with strategic framing of the decision question'
  C_M3_1: 'Explicit per-kilometer cost arithmetic cited (E2, E3)'  (1) ↔ (7)  'Cost framed as relative multiplier only (E1)'
  C_M3_2: 'Frames residual 30% as hard-infrastructure carve-out (E2, E3)'  (1) ↔ (7)  'Frames 30% baseline as absorbing critical needs (E4)'
  C_M3_3: 'Opens with coastline-length feasibility argument (E2, E3)'  (1) ↔ (7)  'Opens with long-horizon compounding-value framing (E5)'
  C_M4_1: 'Leads with cost-per-km arithmetic of hard infrastructure (E1, E4)'  (1) ↔ (7)  'Leads with compounding value under long-horizon uncertainty (E5)'
  C_M4_2: 'Frames 30% baseline as absorbing hard-infrastructure needs (E3, E4)'  (1) ↔ (7)  'Frames decision around temporal compounding logic (E5)'
  C_M4_3: 'Specifies residual 30% allocation toward urban nodes/retreat (E1, E3)'  (1) ↔ (7)  'Justifies primary choice without detailing residual split (E5)'
  C_M5_1: 'Leads with cost-per-km arithmetic of hard infrastructure (E2, E4)'  (1) ↔ (7)  'Leads with long-horizon uncertainty and compounding value (E5)'
  C_M5_2: 'Explicitly earmarks the 30% baseline for hard infrastructure (E1, E2)'  (1) ↔ (7)  'Frames choice around uncertainty without earmarking residual (E5)'
  C_M5_3: 'Quantifies seawall cost as ~3x mangrove alternative (E1, E2)'  (1) ↔ (7)  'Quantifies ecosystem cost as ~30% of seawalls (E5)'

OUTPUT FORMAT (strict). Write exactly 60 lines, no commentary, no markdown fences, no headers. One line per (construct, element) pair, in this exact format:
<construct_id>,<element>,<rating>

Example (showing the format only - your numbers will differ):
C_M1_1,E1,1
C_M1_1,E2,2
C_M1_1,E3,3
C_M1_2,E1,4
...

Constraints:
- rating is an integer from 1 to 7
- iterate all 12 constructs in the order listed, and within each construct all 5 elements in order (E1-E5)
- produce 60 lines total
- output ONLY the lines, nothing before or after
